She is getting used to the new tools.
Welcome to the team, Ana! How are you settling in so far?
Thanks, Bruno. It is a big transition. I used to work in the office every day, so remote is still unfamiliar to me.
That is completely normal. I am used to it now, but honestly, it took me months to get used to the quiet.
Good to hear. What helped you adjust the most?
A clear routine. Once I got used to blocking focus time, the change felt much easier.
That makes sense. I am slowly getting used to writing things down instead of just talking.
Exactly. Small habits, one at a time. Are you used to our stand-up format yet?
Not completely, but I am getting there. Every day it feels less strange.
You are adapting fast, Ana. In a month, this will be your new comfort zone.
I am used to working remotely now.
I am still getting used to the new tools.
It took me a month to settle in, but I feel at home now.
Change is a gradual process, so I give myself time.

Let's talk about change at work. When you start something new -- a new team, a new tool, a new city -- you leave your comfort zone, and that feels strange at first. The important thing to remember is this: nobody is used to a new routine on day one. You get used to it slowly, step by step. I used to think that adapting fast was a sign of talent. Now I think the opposite. The people who thrive are not the ones who never struggle; they are the ones who keep adjusting when things feel unfamiliar. So give yourself time. In the first weeks, everything is harder than it needs to be. But every day, the new becomes a little more normal. That is resilience: not the absence of difficulty, but the habit of adjusting until the unfamiliar becomes your new comfort zone.

Hi Ana, it's Camila from the design team, just a quick welcome note before our first call. I remember my own first month here, so I know how it feels. Honestly, I wasn't used to a fully remote team at all. I used to work in an office every day, with people around me. The first two weeks were the hardest. I couldn't get used to writing everything down instead of just turning to my neighbor. But it got easier, I promise. My advice: don't try to change everything at once. Pick one new habit a week. By the second month, you won't even notice the difference. If you ever feel lost, just message me, we have all been there. You're going to settle in faster than you think.

Here is how Ana settled into her new remote team. First, she accepted that the first weeks would feel unfamiliar. Then, she picked one new habit to adjust to each week. After that, she asked a teammate how they got used to the routine. Next, she practiced the new tools a little every day. Finally, after a month, she felt at home and started to thrive.
